instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Logging Pipeline Architecture

Application logs are emitted as structured JSON via Go's slog package. Each log line includes service_name, request_id, user_id, workspace_id, timestamp, level, and message. Logs are collected by Fluentbit running as a DaemonSet on each Kubernetes node.

Fluentbit forwards logs to Grafana Loki for indexing and querying. We chose Loki over Elasticsearch because Loki only indexes labels (not full text), which dramatically reduces storage costs. Grafana dashboards provide log exploration and alerting.

Retention policy: 30 days for debug/info logs, 90 days for warn/error logs, 1 year for audit logs. Log volume averages 50GB per day across all services. We use Loki's compactor to enforce retention automatically.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use Grafana Loki for Logs"},{"type":"decision","name":"Use Fluentbit Collector"},{"type":"entity","name":"Grafana Loki"},{"type":"entity","name":"Fluentbit"},{"type":"entity","name":"Grafana"},{"type":"entity","name":"slog"},{"type":"entity","name":"Kubernetes"},{"type":"entity","name":"Elasticsearch"},{"type":"concept","name":"Structured Logging"},{"type":"concept","name":"Log Retention"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use Grafana Loki for Logs","target":"Grafana Loki","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use Fluentbit Collector","target":"Fluentbit","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use Fluentbit Collector","target":"Kubernetes","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Structured Logging","target":"slog","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use Grafana Loki for Logs","target":"Structured Logging","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Grafana Loki","target":"Grafana","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Use Grafana Loki for Logs","target":"Log Retention","relation":"affects"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# API Gateway Configuration

We use Kong as our API gateway, deployed in DB-less mode with declarative YAML configuration stored in Git. Kong handles TLS termination, request routing, rate limiting, and authentication plugin execution.

Upstream services register via Kubernetes service discovery. Kong routes based on host header and path prefix. Health checks run every 10 seconds with a 3-failure threshold before marking an upstream unhealthy.

Plugins enabled: jwt (token validation), rate-limiting (per consumer), cors (cross-origin), request-transformer (header injection), and prometheus (metrics export). We chose Kong over Traefik because Kong's plugin ecosystem is more mature and its Lua-based custom plugin system allows us to implement tenant-aware routing logic.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use Kong API Gateway"},{"type":"entity","name":"Kong"},{"type":"entity","name":"Kubernetes"},{"type":"entity","name":"Traefik"},{"type":"entity","name":"Prometheus"},{"type":"entity","name":"Lua"},{"type":"concept","name":"TLS Termination"},{"type":"concept","name":"Service Discovery"},{"type":"concept","name":"Rate Limiting"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use Kong API Gateway","target":"Kong","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use Kong API Gateway","target":"TLS Termination","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use Kong API Gateway","target":"Rate Limiting","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Kong","target":"Kubernetes","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Kong","target":"Prometheus","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Kong","target":"Lua","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Service Discovery","target":"Kubernetes","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
Sprint 22 Retrospective

Shipped: the notification preferences API. Users can now configure per-channel notification settings (email, push, in-app) with granularity per event type. Backend uses PostgreSQL JSONB columns for flexible preference storage. Email delivery switched from SendGrid to Amazon SES for cost savings — volume is 200K emails per month.

Improvement: migrated the background job queue from a custom Redis-based implementation to River (Go library backed by PostgreSQL). This eliminated Redis as a dependency for the notification service and simplified our infrastructure. River provides exactly-once delivery, job scheduling, and a web UI for monitoring.

Tech debt: the webhook delivery system still uses synchronous HTTP calls. Need to make it async with retry logic via River in Sprint 23.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Switch to Amazon SES"},{"type":"decision","name":"Migrate Jobs to River"},{"type":"entity","name":"Amazon SES"},{"type":"entity","name":"SendGrid"},{"type":"entity","name":"River"},{"type":"entity","name":"PostgreSQL"},{"type":"entity","name":"Redis"},{"type":"concept","name":"Notification Preferences"},{"type":"concept","name":"Background Jobs"},{"type":"concept","name":"Webhook Delivery"}],"edges":[{"source":"Switch to Amazon SES","target":"Amazon SES","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Migrate Jobs to River","target":"River","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Migrate Jobs to River","target":"PostgreSQL","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Migrate Jobs to River","target":"Background Jobs","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Notification Preferences","target":"PostgreSQL","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Webhook Delivery","target":"Background Jobs","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Switch to Amazon SES","target":"SendGrid","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Database Backup and Recovery Strategy

PostgreSQL backups use pgBackRest with continuous WAL archiving to Amazon S3. Full backups run weekly (Sunday 02:00 UTC), differential backups run daily. WAL segments are archived every 60 seconds for point-in-time recovery (PITR) capability.

Recovery testing: we restore to a test instance monthly and run a validation suite that checks row counts, foreign key integrity, and application smoke tests. Recovery time objective (RTO) is 30 minutes, recovery point objective (RPO) is 60 seconds.

Encryption: backups are encrypted at rest with AES-256 via pgBackRest's built-in encryption. S3 bucket has versioning enabled and lifecycle rules to move backups to Glacier after 90 days. Total backup storage is approximately 500GB.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use pgBackRest for Backups"},{"type":"decision","name":"Continuous WAL Archiving"},{"type":"entity","name":"pgBackRest"},{"type":"entity","name":"PostgreSQL"},{"type":"entity","name":"Amazon S3"},{"type":"entity","name":"Amazon Glacier"},{"type":"concept","name":"Point-in-Time Recovery"},{"type":"concept","name":"Backup Encryption"},{"type":"concept","name":"Disaster Recovery"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use pgBackRest for Backups","target":"pgBackRest","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use pgBackRest for Backups","target":"Amazon S3","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use pgBackRest for Backups","target":"PostgreSQL","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Continuous WAL Archiving","target":"Point-in-Time Recovery","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use pgBackRest for Backups","target":"Backup Encryption","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use pgBackRest for Backups","target":"Disaster Recovery","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Amazon S3","target":"Amazon Glacier","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Frontend Architecture Decision

We chose Next.js with the App Router for our web dashboard. Server components handle data fetching, client components handle interactivity. State management uses Zustand for client-side state and React Query (TanStack Query) for server state with automatic cache invalidation.

Styling: Tailwind CSS with a custom design token system. Components are built with Radix UI primitives for accessibility. The component library is documented in Storybook.

Authentication: the Next.js middleware validates JWT tokens on every request and refreshes them transparently. API calls use a custom fetch wrapper that handles token refresh, error normalization, and request deduplication. Bundle size is monitored with next-bundle-analyzer and must stay under 200KB for the initial JS payload.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use Next.js App Router"},{"type":"decision","name":"Use Zustand for State"},{"type":"entity","name":"Next.js"},{"type":"entity","name":"Zustand"},{"type":"entity","name":"TanStack Query"},{"type":"entity","name":"Tailwind CSS"},{"type":"entity","name":"Radix UI"},{"type":"entity","name":"Storybook"},{"type":"entity","name":"JWT"},{"type":"concept","name":"Server Components"},{"type":"concept","name":"Design Tokens"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use Next.js App Router","target":"Next.js","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use Next.js App Router","target":"Server Components","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use Zustand for State","target":"Zustand","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Next.js","target":"TanStack Query","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Next.js","target":"Tailwind CSS","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Next.js","target":"Radix UI","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Radix UI","target":"Storybook","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Tailwind CSS","target":"Design Tokens","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Next.js","target":"JWT","relation":"uses"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
Incident Report: Certificate Expiry Outage

Date: 2026-02-20
Severity: P1
Duration: 35 minutes

At 03:15 UTC, all HTTPS traffic to the API started failing with TLS handshake errors. The wildcard certificate for *.api.example.com had expired. cert-manager should have renewed it via Let's Encrypt 30 days before expiry, but the renewal failed silently because the DNS-01 challenge solver lost access to the Route 53 credentials after an IAM policy rotation.

Immediate fix: manually issued a new certificate via certbot. Permanent fix: restored Route 53 credentials for cert-manager, added certificate expiry monitoring in Datadog with a 14-day warning threshold, and created a runbook for manual certificate renewal.

Root cause: IAM credential rotation did not include the cert-manager service account. Added cert-manager to the rotation checklist and infrastructure-as-code in Terraform.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Add Cert Expiry Monitoring"},{"type":"decision","name":"Include cert-manager in IAM Rotation"},{"type":"entity","name":"cert-manager"},{"type":"entity","name":"Let's Encrypt"},{"type":"entity","name":"Route 53"},{"type":"entity","name":"Datadog"},{"type":"entity","name":"Terraform"},{"type":"entity","name":"certbot"},{"type":"concept","name":"TLS Certificate Management"},{"type":"concept","name":"IAM Credential Rotation"},{"type":"concept","name":"Incident Response"}],"edges":[{"source":"Add Cert Expiry Monitoring","target":"Datadog","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Add Cert Expiry Monitoring","target":"TLS Certificate Management","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Include cert-manager in IAM Rotation","target":"Terraform","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Include cert-manager in IAM Rotation","target":"IAM Credential Rotation","relation":"affects"},{"source":"cert-manager","target":"Let's Encrypt","relation":"uses"},{"source":"cert-manager","target":"Route 53","relation":"uses"},{"source":"TLS Certificate Management","target":"Incident Response","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Webhook System Design

Our webhook system delivers events to customer-configured HTTPS endpoints. Events are published to NATS JetStream by the originating service, then consumed by the webhook delivery worker.

Delivery uses exponential backoff with jitter: retries at 1m, 5m, 30m, 2h, 12h. After 5 failures, the webhook is disabled and the customer is notified via email. Each delivery includes an HMAC-SHA256 signature in the X-Webhook-Signature header so recipients can verify authenticity.

The delivery worker is built in Go and uses a worker pool of 50 goroutines. Timeouts: 10 seconds for connection, 30 seconds for response. We log every delivery attempt with status code, latency, and response body (truncated to 1KB) for debugging. Failed deliveries are stored in a dead letter table for manual inspection.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use NATS for Webhooks"},{"type":"decision","name":"HMAC-SHA256 Signatures"},{"type":"entity","name":"NATS JetStream"},{"type":"entity","name":"Go"},{"type":"concept","name":"Webhook Delivery"},{"type":"concept","name":"Exponential Backoff"},{"type":"concept","name":"Dead Letter Queue"},{"type":"concept","name":"Request Signing"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use NATS for Webhooks","target":"NATS JetStream","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use NATS for Webhooks","target":"Webhook Delivery","relation":"affects"},{"source":"HMAC-SHA256 Signatures","target":"Request Signing","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Webhook Delivery","target":"Exponential Backoff","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Webhook Delivery","target":"Dead Letter Queue","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Webhook Delivery","target":"Go","relation":"uses"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Data Pipeline for Analytics

Raw events flow from the API through NATS JetStream into a ClickHouse analytics database. The ingestion worker batches events into 10-second windows before bulk-inserting for throughput. ClickHouse tables use the MergeTree engine with partitioning by date and ordering by workspace_id for efficient tenant-scoped queries.

We run dbt models on a daily schedule via Airflow to transform raw events into materialized analytics views: daily active users, feature usage heatmaps, and funnel analysis. dbt tests validate data quality assertions on every run.

Visualization uses Metabase connected directly to ClickHouse. Dashboards are embedded in our web app via Metabase's iframe embedding with JWT-signed URLs for tenant isolation. We chose ClickHouse over BigQuery for its self-hosted nature and sub-second query performance on columnar data.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use ClickHouse for Analytics"},{"type":"decision","name":"Use dbt for Transforms"},{"type":"entity","name":"ClickHouse"},{"type":"entity","name":"NATS JetStream"},{"type":"entity","name":"dbt"},{"type":"entity","name":"Airflow"},{"type":"entity","name":"Metabase"},{"type":"entity","name":"BigQuery"},{"type":"concept","name":"Analytics Pipeline"},{"type":"concept","name":"Batch Ingestion"},{"type":"concept","name":"Multi-Tenant Isolation"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use ClickHouse for Analytics","target":"ClickHouse","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use ClickHouse for Analytics","target":"Analytics Pipeline","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use dbt for Transforms","target":"dbt","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use dbt for Transforms","target":"Airflow","relation":"uses"},{"source":"NATS JetStream","target":"ClickHouse","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Analytics Pipeline","target":"Batch Ingestion","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Metabase","target":"ClickHouse","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Metabase","target":"Multi-Tenant Isolation","relation":"related_to"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Distributed Tracing Setup

We instrument all Go services with OpenTelemetry using the otel-go SDK. Traces are exported via OTLP gRPC to a Grafana Tempo backend. Each service propagates trace context via W3C Trace Context headers (traceparent, tracestate).

Sampling strategy: head-based sampling at 10% for normal traffic, 100% for error traces (tail-based via the OpenTelemetry Collector). The collector runs as a DaemonSet on Kubernetes and handles sampling, batching, and export.

Custom spans are added for database queries (via otel SQL wrapper), Redis operations, NATS publish/subscribe, and external HTTP calls. Span attributes include workspace_id and user_id for tenant-scoped trace filtering. Grafana dashboards correlate traces with logs (Loki) and metrics (Prometheus) using exemplars.
output: {"nodes":[{"type":"decision","name":"Use OpenTelemetry for Tracing"},{"type":"decision","name":"Use Grafana Tempo Backend"},{"type":"entity","name":"OpenTelemetry"},{"type":"entity","name":"Grafana Tempo"},{"type":"entity","name":"Grafana"},{"type":"entity","name":"Kubernetes"},{"type":"entity","name":"Prometheus"},{"type":"entity","name":"Grafana Loki"},{"type":"entity","name":"Go"},{"type":"concept","name":"Distributed Tracing"},{"type":"concept","name":"Trace Sampling"},{"type":"concept","name":"Observability"}],"edges":[{"source":"Use OpenTelemetry for Tracing","target":"OpenTelemetry","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Use OpenTelemetry for Tracing","target":"Distributed Tracing","relation":"affects"},{"source":"Use Grafana Tempo Backend","target":"Grafana Tempo","relation":"uses"},{"source":"Distributed Tracing","target":"Trace Sampling","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Distributed Tracing","target":"Observability","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Grafana","target":"Grafana Loki","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Grafana","target":"Prometheus","relation":"related_to"},{"source":"Use OpenTelemetry for Tracing","target":"Go","relation":"uses"}]}

instruction: Extract entities and relationships from this developer note. Return JSON.
input:
# Mobile App Architecture

The iOS and Android apps are built with React Native using Expo for managed builds and OTA updates. Navigation uses React Navigation with a bottom tab layout. State management follows the same pattern as web: Zustand for local state, TanStack Query for server state.

Offline support: the app caches the last 100 items per list using MMKV (a fast key-value store). When offline, writes queue in MMKV and sync when connectivity returns via a background task. Conflict resolution uses last-write-wins with server timestamps.

Push notifications use Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) for Android and Apple Push Notification Service (APNs) for iOS. The backend sends push tokens to our notification service which dispatches via the appropriate provider. Deep linking maps notification taps to specific screens using React Navigation's linking config.
